summary: Gruber corrects his earlier implication that Anthropic's Claude was not trained on public web data, clarifying that both Anthropic and Apple use publicly available web content for LLM training. He examines Apple's and Anthropic's training data policies side by side, finding them largely equivalent. His main critique of Apple is the timing problem: they trained on public web data before providing an opt-out mechanism via Applebot-Extended. Ultimately, Gruber argues that using public web data for training is fundamentally acceptable, drawing an analogy to how humans learn from published information. His standard for AI is the same as for people: learn freely, but don't plagiarize — attribute and link.
key_points: Gruber retracts his earlier suggestion that Anthropic didn't train on public web data, showing their FAQ confirms they do
Apple and Anthropic have nearly identical training data policies: public web, licensed datasets, and user-provided data with privacy safeguards
Apple's key misstep was training on public web content before offering publishers an opt-out mechanism for AI crawling
Gruber argues public web data is inherently there to learn from, even by non-human learners, and no LLM exists that wasn't trained on it
His standard for AI mirrors his standard for people: free to learn, not free to plagiarize — attribute and link to sources
money_quotes: The whole point of the public web is that it's there to learn from — even if the learner isn't human.

Is there a single LLM that was not trained on the public web? To my knowledge there is not, and a model that is ignorant of all information available on the public web would be, well, pretty ignorant of the world.
You're free to learn from anything I publish, but not free to plagiarize it. If you quote it, attribute and link to the source. That's my standard for AI bots as well.
It's fair for public data to be excluded on an opt-out basis, rather than included on an opt-in one, but Apple trained its models on the public web before they allowed for opting out.
Clearly, a website that bans Applebot-Extended shouldn't have its data in Apple's training corpus simply because Applebot crawled it before Apple Intelligence was even announced.
So at the moment, my robots.txt file bans just one: Perplexity.
key_insight: Training LLMs on public web data is as legitimate as humans learning from published work — the line isn't at training, it's at plagiarism and proper attribution.
